prediction.attackType: Privilege Escalation (T1548 - Abuse of an Elevated Process to Gain Privileges)
prediction.summary: A low-privilege user, `www-data`, successfully escalated privileges to root on the `app` server. The user then attempted to access sensitive system files (`/etc/shadow`) and created a setuid binary (`/tmp/.svc_bash`) from `/bin/bash`, indicating an attempt to maintain persistent root access or facilitate further malicious activity. A critical SIEM alert was triggered for privilege escalation abuse.

timeline.event: A root session was opened for user `root` by `www-data`.
User `www-data` attempted to read `/etc/shadow` as root.
A `chmod` system call was executed, potentially related to privilege escalation.
Permissions of `/usr/bin/sudo` were noted (mode 04755, ouid 0, ogid 0).
Another root session was opened for user `root` by `www-data`.
Permissions of `/tmp/.svc_bash` were changed to 4755 (setuid) by uid=33 (`www-data`).
An authentication log reported a privilege escalation attempt by `www-data` executing root-level commands.
The root session for user `root` was closed.
A SIEM alert was triggered for critical privilege escalation abuse by `www-data` using technique T1548.

recommendations: Immediately isolate the affected `app` server to prevent further compromise and contain the incident.
Investigate the initial vector that allowed the `www-data` user to execute commands as root (e.g., vulnerable web application, misconfigured sudoers file, exploited service).
Analyze the `/tmp/.svc_bash` binary for malicious code or its intended use by the attacker. Remove it and any other unauthorized files.
Review all user activity for `www-data` prior to and during the incident for any other suspicious actions or persistence mechanisms.
Change all credentials associated with the `app` server and any potentially compromised accounts.
Perform a full forensic analysis of the system to identify the extent of the breach and any data exfiltration.
